evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go defines large callback-bag abstractions (`Runtime`, `StartupRuntime`, `StartramRuntime`, `dockerRuntime` families) plus many `With*` option functions that only copy non-nil function pointers field-by-field.

goseg/startup_orchestrator.go repeats the same nil-guarded callback wiring pattern in `startupRuntime` and `withDefaults`, and tests in goseg/startup_orchestrator_test.go primarily validate callback presence rather than domain behavior.
goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go exports many package-level functions that only forward to `defaultRuntime` methods, creating duplicated call surfaces with little additional policy or translation.

goseg/config/config_view.go introduces a typed `ConfigView` abstraction, but assigned modules still frequently bypass it via direct global reads (`config.Conf`, `config.UrbitConf`, `config.GetStartramConfig`) in goseg/rectify/rectify.go, goseg/routines/healthcheck/disk.go, and goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations.go.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes create high indirection cost without clear domain boundaries.
evidence: `runtime_seams.go` composes many function-pointer structs and option mutators (`WithContainerOps`, `WithUrbitOps`, `WithSnapshotOps`, etc.) that mostly perform nil checks and assignment.
`startup_orchestrator.go` `startupRuntime` and `withDefaults` repeat the same field-by-field callback override pattern across many members.
`operations_runtime.go` repeats the callback-bag pattern (`workflowRuntime`) with defensive nil checks and sentinel errors for missing callbacks.
Tests in `startup_orchestrator_test.go` include substantial configuration of callback bags, signaling high setup burden from abstraction shape.
findings.suggestion: Replace callback bags with smaller, role-focused interfaces (for example bootstrapping, transition emit, container lifecycle) and require dependencies via constructors so missing behavior fails at compile time instead of runtime nil checks.

findings.summary: Package-level forwarders duplicate APIs around singleton runtimes and add low-leverage abstraction layers.
evidence: `lifecycle.go` defines many package functions (`Initialize`, `GetShipStatus`, `StartContainer`, `DeleteContainer`, etc.) that simply call `defaultRuntime` methods.
`operations_runtime.go` public helpers repeatedly call `defaultWorkflowRuntime()` and then delegate into internal `...WithRuntime` variants, creating parallel entry paths.
`main.go` adds additional runtime wrapping (`bootstrapRuntime`, `bootstrapRuntimeFns`) that mirrors this forwarding style.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one boundary style per subsystem: either explicit runtime instances passed from composition roots or direct package functions; remove redundant dual surfaces where wrappers do not enforce extra policy.

findings.summary: The typed config view abstraction is not consistently used, so modules bypass it and couple directly to the global config bag.
evidence: `config_view.go` defines `ConfigView` and snapshot methods intended to expose narrower typed slices of config state.
`rectify.go` directly reads and mutates through `config.Conf`, `config.GetContainerState`, and `config.UpdateConfTyped` inside transition handlers.
`disk.go` loops fetch full `config.Conf()` and then read/write warning and pier data directly from `SysConfig` shape.
`urbit_operations.go` repeatedly calls `config.UrbitConf`/`config.GetContainerState` inline across workflow operations instead of using a narrowed view abstraction.
`structs/configs.go` `SysConfig` is a very wide aggregate, increasing accidental coupling when used as a general-purpose dependency.
findings.suggestion: Enforce `ConfigView` (or smaller purpose-built readers/writers) at orchestration boundaries, and phase out direct `config.Conf`/`config.UrbitConf` access in workflow and routine packages to reduce global bag coupling.
